content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES CoMIIsSIoN
P.O. Box 807
• 428 .1 Street
Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
June 15, 1999
Dma P. Gartland
City of Huntington Beach
Public Works Commission
19192 Shoreline Drive, #8
Huntington Beach, California 92648
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-114
Dear Ms. Gartland:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Since your request asks for general
guidance, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
Please note that this letter is solely based on the facts presented to us in your letter, and in
the telephone conversation that I had with you on May 17, 1999. The Commission does not act
as a finder of fact when issuing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Our advice is
applicable only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material
facts have been provided.
Additionally, you should keep in mind that the Commission’s jurisdiction is limited to
the provisions of the Act. We therefore cannot advise you regarding the impact that other laws,
such as the doctrine of incompatible activities, may have on your situation.
3
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; and Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
For advice about whether the doctrine of incompatible activities applies to your situation, you should
contact your city attorney.


File No. 1-99-114
Page 2
QUESTIONS
1. Does the Act prohibit the engineering firm that employs you from bidding on public
works projects while you are a member of the Huntington Beach Public Works Commission?
2. Does the Act prohibit you from voting on public works projects that are being bid on
by your employer?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The Act does not prohibit your employer from bidding on public works projects while
you are a member of the Huntington Beach Public Works Commission.
2. The Act prohibits you from voting on, or otherwise participating in, any decision by
the Huntington Beach Public Works Commission, about a public works project, that will have a
material financial effect on your employer, that is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.
FACTS
You are one of seven appointed members of the Public Works Commission for the City
ofHuntington Beach (the “Public Works Commission”). In addition to being a member of the
Public Works Commission, you are also an employee of an engineering firm (the “firm”). The
firm is frequently involved in designing public works projects. You receive an annual salary
from the firm, but you have no investment interest in it.
The purpose of the Public Works Commission is to provide advice and recommendations
to the Huntington Beach City Council (the “City Council”) on matters related to public works.
As a member of the Public Works Commission, you are required to complete an annual
statement of economic interests (Form 700).
You would like to know whether the Act prohibits the firm from bidding on public works
projects in Huntington Beach while you are a member of the Public Works Commission. You
would also like to know whether the Act prohibits you from voting on public works projects that
are being bid on by the firm.
ANALYSIS
The conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit a public official from malcing,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the public official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she
has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Whenever a public official has a financial interest in a
governmental decision, the official is required by the Act to abstain from participation in the
decision. (Strickland Advice Letter, No. 1-91-046.)


File No. 1-99-114
Page 3
Question 1
The disqualification requirement imposed by the Act is strictly limited to persons who
satisfy the Act’s definition for a “public official.”
The tenn “public official” is defined by Section 82048 of thç Act, as follows:
“Public official’ means every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency, but does not
include judges and court commissioners in the judicial branch of
government.”
The firm that employs you clearly does not satisfy the Act’s definition for a public
official. As such, the Act does not prohibit the firm from participating in any governmental
decisions, including participating as a bidder in decisions regarding public works projects. The
finn is therefore free to bid on public works projects for the City ofHuntington Beach, regardless
ofyour position as a member of the Public Works Commission.
Question 2
Public Official
Whether or not you satisfy the Act’s definition for a public official is dependent upon
whether, as a member of the Public Works Commission, you qualify as a member of a
governmental agency. Regulation 18701(a)(1) provides that “member” of a governmental
agency
4 includes salaried or unsalaried members of a board or commission with decisionmaking
authority.
In your letter requesting advice, you indicated that you are required to complete an annual
statement of economic interests (Form 700). This requirement could only be present if the
Public Works Commission, and the Huntington Beach City Council, have previously determined
that the Public Works Commission is a decisionmaking body, and you hold a decisionmaking
position. (Sections 87300
- 87310.) In view of that determination, we must conclude that you
are a public official, and therefore subject to the disqualification requirements of the Act.
‘ Under Section 82041, a local governmental agency is defmed as “a county, city, or district of any kind
including school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, or any department, division, bureau,
office, board, commission or other agency of the foregoing.”


File No. 1-99-1 14
Page 4
Making, Participating in Making, Or Attempting To Use Your Official Position To
Influence a Governmental Decision
Regulations 18702 - 18702.3 set forth the standards for determining when a public
official is making, participating in maldng, or attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision. A public official “makes a governmental decision,” when,
among other things, the official votes on a matter, appoints a person, commits his or her agency
to a course of conduct, enters into a contract on behalf of his or her agency, or determines not to
act in one of the foregoing manners. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official “participates in a
governmental decision,” when, among other things, the official negotiates with a governmental
entity, or advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker. (Regulation 18702.2.) A
public official “is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision” by the official’s agency, or an agency that is appointed by, or subject to the budgetary
control ofhis or her agency, when, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee,
or consultant of the agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
By application of these standards, you would be making, participating in making, or
attempting to use your official position to influence a governmental decision if you vote on, or
otherwise participate in, a decision by the Public Works Commission, about a public works
project.
Economic Interests
Whether you have a financial interest in a decision is governed by Section 87103, which
provides, in part, that:
“A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his
or her immediate family, or on any of the following:
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of
business on terms available to the public without regard to official
status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in
value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management.”


File No. 1-99-114
Page 5
In your letter requesting advice, you stated that you are an employee of an engineering
firm. You therefore have a potentially disqualifying economic interest in the firm under Sections
87103(c) and (d).
Once a public official’s economic interests have been identified, it is necessary to
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a governmental decision will have a material
financial effect on any of the economic interests that have been identified. There are three steps
to making this evaluation. First, it must be determined whether the official’s economic interests
will be directly or indirectly involved in the decision. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(4).) Second, the
appropriate standard must be selected for determining whether the financial impact of the
decision on any particular economic interest will be material. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(5).) Third,
it must be determined whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be
satisfied for any particular economic interest. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(6).) If it is substantially
likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of the official’s economic interests,
then the public official will have a conflict of interest, unless the “public generally exception”
applies. If it is not substantially likely that the materiality standard will be satisfied for any of the
official’s economic interests, then the public official will not have a conflict of interest. We
stress that this is a case-by-case determination.
Direct Versus Indirect Involvement
Regulation 18704.1 sets forth the criteria for detenuining whether an economic interest is
directly or indirectly involved in a decision. This regulation states:
“(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income,
and sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an
official’s agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made
by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official’s agency.
A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person.”
The firm would be the subject of a decision regarding a public works project that is being
bid on by the firm, because such a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or
revocation of a contract with the firm. As such, the firm would be directly involved in that
decision.


File No. 1-99-114
Page 6
The Appropriate Materiality Standard
Regulation 18705.3 prescribes the rules for assessing whether an official’s economic
interest in a source of income is materially affected by a decision. Under subsection (a) of this
regulation, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a
public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official’s agency, is deemed
material. By application of this regulation, if a governmental decision regarding a public works
project that directly involves the firm will have any effect at all on the firm, that effect will be
deemed material. This is a “one-penny” rule.
Foreseeability
Whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision regarding a public works project that
directly involves the firm will have some effect on the firm is the critical question in this
analysis. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is
made if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.) Certainty is not
required. Only if an effect is just a mere possibility, is it not reasonably foreseeable. (Downey
Cares v. Downey Development Corn. (1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983,989-991; Witt v. Morrow
(1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817, 822; and In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
You have not provided us with any information about any specific governmental
decisions regarding public works projects that you will be making. We are therefore unable to
complete this part of the analysis for any particular decision. You will have to do this for
yourself, based upon the information that is available to you, at the time a decision is about to be
made.
It should be noted, however, that it appears highly unlikely that any governmental
decision about a public works project on which the firm is bidding will have no reasonably
foreseeable economic effect on the firm. We must therefore presume that any governmental
decision about a public works project on which the firm is bidding will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the firm.
Public Generally
Upon reaching the conclusion that a governmental decision regarding a public works
project on which the firm is bidding will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the firm, you may not be involved in that decision, unless the effect on the firm’s economic
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. For the “public generally”
exception to apply to a decision, the decision must affect the firm in substantially the same
manner as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18707.)
Regulation 18707(b)(1)(B) defines the term “significant segment,” as it relates to an
economic interest in a business entity, as “flfiy percent of all businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents, so long as the segment is composed of persons other than a single
industry, trade, or profession.”


File No. 1-99-1 14
Page 7
Under the facts presented to us, it appears unlikely that a decision by the Public Worls
Commission regarding a public works project that is being bid on by the firm would affect fifty
percent of all businesses in Huntington Beach, or any particular district in Huntington Beach that
you may represent, in substantially the same manner as the firm. Nonetheless, you must make
this determination in order to complete the analysis ofwhether you may participate in the
decision.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Steven Benito Russo
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

by your employer?

generally.

by your employer?

CONCLUSION: 1. The Act does not prohibit your employer from bidding on public works projects while
you are a member of the Huntington Beach Public Works Commission.
2. The Act prohibits you from voting on, or otherwise participating in, any decision by
the Huntington Beach Public Works Commission, about a public works project, that will have a
